summary: The largest pre-Columbian empire in the Americas, centered at Cuzco in the Andes and known to its people as Tawantinsuyu, the "Land of the Four Quarters." Clues most often identify it through its capital city (laid out in the shape of a puma), its knotted-string record-keeping system called quipu, its road network traveled by chasqui runners, and its conquest by the Spaniard Francisco Pizarro after the capture of the ruler Atahualpa at Cajamarca. The empire was massively expanded from the small Kingdom of Cuzco by the ruler Pachacuti in the fifteenth century and collapsed after a civil war between Huascar and Atahualpa left it vulnerable to Spanish conquest in the 1530s, with a rump state surviving at Vilcabamba until 1572.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Clues most commonly identify the empire through its capital of Cuzco (reportedly laid out in the shape of a puma), its self-designation Tawantinsuyu and division into four suyus, its quipu record-keeping system, and its conquest by Francisco Pizarro. Machu Picchu, its most famous surviving site, is frequently cited as a giveaway tied to the ruler Pachacuti, and the empire is regularly described as stretching along the Andes from Ecuador/Colombia to Chile.

clues.clue: Its capital, Cuzco (Cusco), was reportedly laid out in the shape of a puma, with the city's tail formed by two rivers
examples: This civilization built their capital city in the shape of a puma, whose tail is formed by two rivers.
A holder of this title constructed the Sacsayhuaman fortress in a city which was laid out in the shape of a puma.

clues.clue: Called itself Tawantinsuyu, the "Land of the Four Quarters/Corners," divided into four suyus that converged on Cuzco
examples: This empire used that labor to build a vast road network that connected its namesake "Four Regions," or Tawantinsuyu, from the puna grasslands of Ecuador to Chile.
People with this title ruled the Land of Four Quarters named for each cardinal direction, which were called suyu in their language of Quechua.

clues.clue: Kept records on knotted-string devices called quipu (khipu), which were later burned en masse by Spanish colonial authorities
examples: This empire used a record system called quipu that consisted of knotted ropes.
kept records on knotted-string devices burned en masse during colonial times, called khipus.

clues.clue: Was conquered by the Spanish conquistador Francisco Pizarro
examples: name this South American empire that included Cuzco and Machu Picchu and was conquered by Francisco Pizarro.
Name this empire centered in modern-day Peru. It was conquered by the Spanish in 1572.

clues.clue: Its most famous site, Machu Picchu, was built as an estate for the ruler Pachacuti
examples: name this Andean empire whose site of Machu Picchu was built for its ruler Pachacuti.
Most archaeologists believe that this Inca site on the Andes was built as an estate for Pachacuti.

clues.clue: Spoke Quechua and stretched along the Andes from roughly Ecuador to Chile
examples: name this empire that spoke Quechua and was based at Cusco.
name this empire that ruled much of Western South America in pre-Columbian times.

works.name: Origins and the Founding of Cuzco
works.description: The empire's origin legend describes the sun god Inti sending Manco Capac -- accompanied by his sister/wife Mama Ocllo and, in longer versions, three other "Ayar Brothers" and four sisters -- to found a city wherever a golden staff sank into the ground. That site became Cuzco, and Manco Capac became the first Sapa Inca. Rival brothers were lost along the way, leaving Manco Capac the sole founder.

clues.clue: The sun god Inti sent Manco Capac (and, in some tellings, taught him alongside his sister Mama Ocllo) to found a city where a golden staff would sink into the ground -- the future Cuzco
examples: The first ruler of these people supposedly emerged from a cave with four sisters and three other "Ayar Brothers" and founded a city where a sun god's staff sank into the ground.
Inti later taught this legendary founder of the Inca empire, along with his daughter Mama Ocllo, how to build civilization.

clues.clue: Manco Capac emerged with three other "Ayar Brothers" and four sisters from a cave/window at Tambo Toco, and became sole founder after his brothers were lost along the journey (one grew wings and flew away, another was sealed in a cave, two turned to stone)
examples: A brother of a ruler of these people spontaneously grew wings and flew to the "Sun Settlement" after another brother was sealed away for destroying too many mountains with a sling.
Two of the Ayar Brothers, Ayar Uchu and Ayar Auca, experienced this fate while travelling to Cuzco.

clues.clue: Manco Capac became the first holder of the title Sapa Inca and was traditionally considered a son of the sun god Inti
examples: Manco Capac was the first ruler of - for 10 points - what people, whose empire's capital was Cuzco.
The first person to hold this title was believed to be the son of the sun god Inti.

works.name: Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui
works.description: Pachacuti is credited with transforming the small Kingdom of Cuzco into the Inca Empire proper after repelling an invasion by the Chanka people. He reorganized the state into Tawantinsuyu, built the empire's most iconic monuments including Machu Picchu and the Coricancha sun temple, and began the conquest of the neighboring Chimu, work later finished by his son.

clues.clue: Repelled a Chanka invasion of Cuzco -- legend holds stones turned into soldiers to help him -- after his father or brother fled the besieged city; he then seized the royal fringe from his brother Urco in a power struggle
examples: This king made his father wear women's clothing and drink beer from a dirty pot after seizing the royal tassel in a power struggle with his brother Urco.
He sought revenge on the Chankas for their siege of Cusco, in which this Inca bravely ran away, leaving the ...

clues.clue: Took the name Pachacuti, meaning "Earthshaker" or "he who overturns/reverses the world"
examples: another earned a name meaning "overturner of the world" or "earthshaker"
A holder of this title gave himself a name meaning "Reverser of the World," reflecting the belief in the regular "turning over of time and space" his culture held.

clues.clue: Transformed the Kingdom of Cuzco into the Inca Empire, reorganizing it into Tawantinsuyu and reportedly introducing the hanan/hurin social classes and the mitma resettlement policy
examples: This king, who supposedly introduced the hanan and hurin social classes, is also credited with forcibly moving populations between the four suyus of his realm in the first instance of the mitma policy.
Name this ruler, who reorganized his empire into the Tawantinsuyu, or "Land of the Four Corners."

clues.clue: Built the Coricancha ("Golden Enclosure") sun temple, commissioning life-sized gold statues of animals and plants for its garden
examples: This king commissioned life-sized gold statues of animals and plant for the central garden of his Coricancha temple.

clues.clue: Began the conquest of the Chimu people, work later completed by his son Topa Inca Yupanqui
examples: Much of the land of the Chimu people was conquered by what king who built Machu Picchu and transformed the Kingdom of Cuzco into the Inca Empire?
This empire had its capital at Chan Chan and worshipped a moon god.

clues.clue: In the play Ollantay, the title general rebels after being forbidden from marrying Pachacuti's daughter Cusi Coyllur
examples: The title character stages a rebellion after being forbidden from marrying this king's daughter Cusi Coyllur in the play Ollantay.

works.name: Society, Administration, and Economy
works.description: The empire organized labor and land through kinship-based ayllu communities headed by local curacas, and extracted labor through the mit'a rotational-service tax rather than monetary tribute. Conquered peoples were sometimes forcibly resettled (mitma) to secure new territory, and after an emperor's death his mummy and estate were maintained by a royal kin group called a panaka. The state relied on freeze-dried food storage and elite female labor (the aclla) for its religious and administrative functions.

clues.clue: Organized labor and land through kinship groups called ayllu, whose local head was the curaca, in charge of divvying up land and labor
examples: This empire's ayllu clan structure organized the citizens who paid taxes in-kind via labor through its mit'a system.
This was the title given to the head of each ayllu in the Incan Empire.

clues.clue: Required rotational labor service called mit'a for public works such as roads, storehouses, and temples, rather than monetary taxation
examples: People in this empire were subject to work on public projects in the mit'a system.
The first empire to use the Mit'a system was this one, which controlled most of the Andes before its conquest in 1534.

clues.clue: Resettled conquered populations (a policy called mitma) into new regions, replacing them with loyal subjects, relocating an estimated 25-30% of its total population
examples: This non-Middle Eastern state controlled restive peoples by forcing their chiefs to live at the capital and deporting them to new regions, placing loyal subjects in their place.
This empire is estimated to have relocated 25-30% of its population.

clues.clue: After an emperor's death, his mummy and property were maintained by a panaka, a royal kin group that also controlled private armies and fueled succession disputes
examples: The Inca Empire's government was dominated by these familial organizations founded upon each emperor's death, which administrated his properties, controlled private armies, and were a frequent way for...

clues.clue: Selected girls called aclla ("Chosen Women") to weave and brew chicha beer in state and religious service; those chosen were expected to remain celibate before marriage
examples: Though celibacy before marriage was not expected of most girls in this empire, it was expected for those chosen to weave and brew beer as aclla.
Regional officials called apupanakas selected girls to be sent to the capital where the ruler of this empire could choose them to become aclla.

clues.clue: Preserved food by freeze-drying, producing chuno from potatoes and ch'arki (source of the English word "jerky") from meat, stored in hillside storehouses called qollqa
examples: Preserved potatoes called chuno and ch'arky, inspiring the word "jerky," were eaten by this empire's residents.
this empire placed storehouses called qollqa on hillsides, which were visited by chasquis.

clues.clue: Experimented with agriculture using diverse ecological zones, including the terraced site of Moray, likened by scholar John Victor Murra to a "vertical archipelago" system of exploiting multiple altitudes
examples: This empire likely used the site of Moray as a "laboratory garden" to develop hardier strains of staple foods.
Leftist historian John Victor Murra called this empire's trade and agricultural system a "Vertical archipelago".

works.name: The Inca Road System
works.description: The empire's road network, known as Qhapaq Nan, ran roughly 25,000 miles and linked the far reaches of Tawantinsuyu through the capital at Cuzco. It was built and maintained through mit'a labor, traveled by chasqui relay runners, and lined with inns, storehouses, and rope suspension bridges -- infrastructure so heavily and repeatedly clued that it functions almost as its own answerline.

clues.clue: Known as Qhapaq Nan, the roughly 25,000-mile network linked Quito to Mendoza (through Chile) via the capital Cuzco, and was built and maintained through mit'a labor
examples: This system was demarcated in units called topo and known in the local language as Qhapaq Nan, and this system's largest portion was built at Huanuco Pampa.
The Inca used mit'a labor to construct the rope bridges which connected sections of this 25,000-mile-long system together.

clues.clue: Chasqui runners relayed messages (including quipu) along the road, resting at tampu/tambo inns spaced about a day's walk apart and blowing horns called pututus to announce their approach
examples: The most frequent users of this infrastructure system blew on horns called pututus to announce their coming.
This infrastructure system was lined with inns called tambos and warehouses called qullqas.

clues.clue: Crossed gorges with suspension bridges woven from ichu grass, such as the Q'iswa Chaka -- rebuilt annually and still standing in the village of Huinchiri -- or with basket-and-cable crossings called oroyas
examples: A notable part of this system is the Q'iswa Chaka, which is rebuilt every June out of ichhu grass.
An alternative to crossing gorges by bridge was by using one of these objects called an oroya.

clues.clue: Its most-clued surviving stretch, near Machu Picchu, passes a site whose name translates to "dead woman"
examples: The most famous section of this infrastructure system passes through an area whose name translates to "dead woman".

clues.clue: Was marked with ritual cairns called apacheta and connected sacred shrine sites called wak'a
examples: Some stretches of this system were marked with cairns that denoted ritual waypoints called apacheta.
This system served to connect sacred sites called wak'as.

works.name: The Inca Civil War
works.description: A war of succession between the half-brothers Huascar and Atahualpa broke out after their father Huayna Capac died of smallpox alongside his intended heir. Atahualpa's generals ultimately won the war, but Atahualpa himself was captured by the Spanish at Cajamarca soon after his victory, leaving the empire without effective leadership at the moment of conquest.

clues.clue: Fought between the half-brothers Huascar and Atahualpa after their father Huayna Capac and his intended heir Ninan Cuyochi both died in a smallpox epidemic, triggering a succession crisis
examples: It began after both the death of Ninan Cuyochi and his father due to a smallpox epidemic.
This Incan ruler lost a civil war to his half-brother Atahualpa, after their father Huayna Capac's death from smallpox led to a succession crisis.

clues.clue: Huayna Capac reportedly dreamed of thousands of foreigners standing on an empty battlefield shortly before his death
examples: One of the final rulers of this empire dreamt of thousands of foreigners on an empty battlefield just before his death, which precipitated a civil war between his two sons.

clues.clue: Atahualpa's generals Chalcuchimac and Quizquiz won the war for him; the defeated enemy general Atoc reportedly had his skull turned into a drinking cup and his skin made into a drum
examples: The winning side in this war employed the skilled generals Quizquiz and Chalcuchimac.
After a battle in this war, a losing general named Atoc either had his skull turned into a drinking cup or had his skin used for a drum.

clues.clue: Huascar's side temporarily captured Atahualpa's forces at Tumebamba after the Canari people defected to Huascar, before ultimately losing the war
examples: The losing side in this war temporarily captured the enemy monarch at Tumebamba after the Canari leaders defected from his side.

clues.clue: Atahualpa won the civil war but was captured by Francisco Pizarro at Cajamarca soon afterward
examples: The winner of this war would himself be captured by foreigners at the Battle of Cajamarca and later executed despite filling a Ransom Room with gold.

works.name: Spanish Conquest and the Fall of the Empire
works.description: The Spanish conquest opened with Francisco Pizarro's ambush and capture of Atahualpa at Cajamarca in 1532. Despite paying an enormous ransom, Atahualpa was executed the following year, and the empire's collapse continued through a puppet-ruler rebellion and a long-surviving rump state at Vilcabamba, which fell only in 1572.

clues.clue: At Cajamarca in 1532, friar Vicente de Valverde offered Atahualpa a Bible, which he threw to the ground, giving the Spanish a pretext to ambush and capture him despite being outnumbered roughly forty to one
examples: When Vicente de Valverde offered a holder of this title the Bible, he threw it to the floor.
his troops routed despite outnumbering the enemy over forty to one.

clues.clue: Atahualpa offered to fill a room once with gold and twice with silver as ransom, but Pizarro had him executed anyway (1533), reportedly after Atahualpa had ordered the killing of his brother Huascar
examples: One ruler of this empire attempted to buy his freedom by filling his jail cell once with gold and twice with silver; that man was Atahualpa.
Despite paying the largest ransom in history, that ruler was killed in this country after ordering the death of his brother Huascar.

clues.clue: The installed puppet ruler Manco Inca was imprisoned by Pizarro's brothers, escaped, and besieged Cuzco for ten months before retreating to found the rump state of Vilcabamba in 1537
examples: This monarch was captured by Spanish forces and imprisoned by the brothers of Francisco Pizarro before escaping and laying siege to Cusco for 10 months.
Vilcabamba was founded by this Incan monarch.

clues.clue: Vilcabamba was the last Inca holdout; its final ruler, Tupac Amaru, was captured and executed when the Spanish finally conquered it in 1572
examples: The last remnant of an empire based in this country was destroyed when the Spanish conquered Vilcabamba and killed Tupac Amaru.
Name this empire centered in modern-day Peru. It was conquered by the Spanish in 1572.

works.name: The Sapa Inca and State Religion
works.description: The emperor's title, Sapa Inca, roughly translates as "the only emperor" and came with distinctive gold and feather regalia. The state religion centered on the sun god Inti, whose worship was maintained at the Coricancha temple in Cuzco by the Virgins of the Sun, and included the child-sacrifice ritual known as capac hucha and the annual Situa purification festival.

clues.clue: The emperor held the title Sapa Inca, meaning roughly "the only emperor," and wore a braided crown of gold tubes with a scarlet fringe, represented in art with four black feathers
examples: Rulers of this polity were known as "the only emperor", and were represented in art by four black feathers fanning from a point.
Holders of this title were given a braided crown made with gold tubes and a scarlet fringe.

clues.clue: Worshipped the sun god Inti as chief state deity and believed him the ancestor of the Sapa Inca; his wife/sister Mama Quilla was the moon goddess
examples: This Incan sun god resided in the hanan pacha. His wife, Mama Quilla, cried tears of silver and served as goddess of the moon.

clues.clue: Maintained the Coricancha ("Golden Enclosure") sun temple at Cuzco, where Virgins of the Sun tended a sacred fire
examples: They built a magnificent temple to their sun god called the "Golden Enclosure."
Some young women in this culture known as Virgins of the Sun tended to the sun god's sacred fire.

clues.clue: Practiced capac hucha, the ritual sacrifice of chosen children at high-altitude shrines, producing frozen mummies such as "La Doncella" and "Mummy Juanita"
examples: Girls now known as La Doncella and Mummy Juanita were sacrificed as part of this empire's ritual of capac hucha.

clues.clue: Celebrated the Situa Festival, an annual purification ("spring cleaning") rite at Cuzco
examples: Identify this empire that practiced an early form of spring cleaning in its capital, Cusco, during the Situa Festival.
This emperor of the Inca wrote special hymns for the Situa Festival.

works.name: Legacy and Historiography
works.description: Much of what is known about the empire comes from early colonial-era chroniclers, including two writers of mixed and Indigenous heritage who defended Inca history and rights. Modern scholarship and popular culture have continued to grapple with the empire's fall, from academic debates over its economic organization to its use as a symbol in Peru today.

clues.clue: Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala's illustrated, roughly 1,400-page letter, The First New Chronicle and Good Government, denounced Spanish mistreatment of the Inca
examples: A history of this empire is structured as a 1400-page letter to a monarch who never read it and is titled New Chronicle and Good Government.
The downfall of this empire is described in the middle sections of The First New Chronicle and Good Government.

clues.clue: Garcilaso de la Vega, son of a Spanish conquistador and an Inca noblewoman, wrote the Royal Commentaries of the Incas (Comentarios Reales), considered the fullest early account and partly composed in Quechua
examples: A history of this empire is the best-known work of the non-poet Garcilaso de la Vega.
Name these people, who were documented in a set of Cometarios Reales by the mestizo author Garcilaso de la Vega, which he wrote in Spanish and Quechua.

clues.clue: Pedro Sarmiento de Gamboa compiled a History of the Incas under Viceroy Francisco de Toledo
examples: Under orders from Peruvian viceroy Francisco de Toleda, this Spaniard composed his monumental History of the Incas, which chronicled the lineage and the mythology of the Incan emperors.

clues.clue: Anthropologist John Victor Murra's scholarship on the empire's ecological economy coined the term "vertical archipelago"
examples: John Victor Murra coined the term "vertical archipelago" to describe agricultural economics in this empire.
A historian of this empire born in Odessa mentored feminist Laura X and adopted a surname meaning "blackberry" in Romanian.

clues.clue: A painting depicting the ruler's capture appears on the cover of Jared Diamond's Guns, Germs, and Steel, which controversially attributes the empire's fall largely to Old World technological and disease advantages
examples: A painting depicting the capture of a ruler of this empire appears on the cover of Guns, Germs, and Steel.
Jared Diamond has been heavily criticized for his explanation of what empire's defeat at Cajamarca?

clues.clue: The Peruvian soft drink Inca Kola is named for the empire
examples: Inca Kola is named for a former empire in this South American nation whose national drink is created by combining pisco with lime juice, syrup, and eggs.

clues.clue: The Inkarri legend, inspired by the later colonial-era rebel Tupac Amaru II and by Atahualpa, holds that the Inca will one day return
examples: An empire ruled by holders of this title will return, per the Inkarri legend inspired by Tupac Amaru II and Atahualpa.

comprehensive_summary: The Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu, "Land of the Four Quarters") was the largest empire in the pre-Columbian Americas, centered on the capital city of Cuzco, which was reportedly laid out in the shape of a puma. According to its origin legend, the sun god Inti sent Manco Capac -- accompanied by his sister/wife Mama Ocllo and, in fuller tellings, three other "Ayar Brothers" and four sisters -- to found a city wherever a golden staff sank into the earth; that site became Cuzco, and Manco Capac became the first holder of the imperial title Sapa Inca, "the only emperor." The empire proper was created out of the small Kingdom of Cuzco by the ruler Pachacuti, who repelled a Chanka invasion (aided, by legend, by stones that turned into soldiers), seized power from his brother Urco, took a name meaning "Earthshaker," and reorganized the state into Tawantinsuyu's four suyus while introducing policies such as mitma resettlement. Pachacuti built the empire's signature monuments, including Machu Picchu and the Coricancha ("Golden Enclosure") sun temple, and began the conquest of the Chimu, completed by his son Topa Inca Yupanqui. Imperial society was organized around ayllu kinship groups administered by curacas, funded by mit'a rotational labor rather than monetary tax, and served by elite women called aclla who wove and brewed chicha. The empire preserved food by freeze-drying it into chuno and ch'arki (source of the word "jerky"), stored supplies in qollqa storehouses, and communicated via knotted-string quipu records carried by chasqui runners along the roughly 25,000-mile Inca road system (Qhapaq Nan), which was lined with tambo inns and crossed by rope suspension bridges like the Q'iswa Chaka. State religion centered on the sun god Inti, whose sacred fire was tended by the Virgins of the Sun at the Coricancha, alongside rituals such as capac hucha child sacrifice and the annual Situa purification festival. The empire's collapse began when Huayna Capac and his intended heir died of smallpox, triggering the Inca Civil War between his sons Huascar and Atahualpa; Atahualpa's generals Chalcuchimac and Quizquiz won the war, but Atahualpa himself was captured by Francisco Pizarro at Cajamarca in 1532 after throwing to the ground a Bible offered by the friar Vicente de Valverde. Despite paying an enormous ransom of gold and silver, Atahualpa was executed in 1533, and Spanish rule was contested for decades afterward: the puppet ruler Manco Inca rebelled, besieged Cuzco, and founded the rump state of Vilcabamba, which held out until the Spanish captured and executed its last ruler, Tupac Amaru, in 1572. Much of what is known of the empire comes from colonial-era chroniclers, notably Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala's illustrated New Chronicle and Good Government and Garcilaso de la Vega's Royal Commentaries of the Incas, while modern legacy ranges from the anthropologist John Victor Murra's "vertical archipelago" model of Andean agriculture to the empire's contested place on the cover of Jared Diamond's Guns, Germs, and Steel and in Peru's Inkarri return legend.

links.text: Felipe Guaman Poma de Ayala -- Wikipedia

recursive_suggestions: Pachacuti -- own recurring bonus answerline (4x) with a dense, independent biographical cluster; could support a standalone ruler page.
Atahualpa -- heavily clued independently around the civil war and Cajamarca; could eventually stand alone if question volume grows.

Inca mythology -- surfaced as its own separate answerline (and several spelling variants) with dense, independent religious/cosmological clue content (Viracocha, Inti, Mama Quilla, the three pachas, creation myths); excluded here as a distinct Mythology-category topic, not folded into this History page beyond brief state-religion facts.
Garcilaso de la Vega -- chronicler with independent literary/historical presence (Royal Commentaries of the Incas); could support a Literature or History figure page.

Ambiguity flag: the search term "Inca" pulled in large numbers of unrelated substring matches (Jamaica Kincaid, Henri/Raymond/Jules Henri Poincare, incandescent light bulbs, incarceration/prisons/mass incarceration, incandescence-adjacent terms, James Orin Incandenza) -- all excluded as false positives from substring matching, not topic overlap.

Ambiguity flag: "Andes Mountains" and "Peru" surfaced as broader geography answerlines with some Inca-specific content folded in; Inca-specific facts from those groups were pulled into this analysis, but a future Geography pass on the Andes/Peru should avoid re-duplicating this empire's core clues.
